summary: Joel Spolsky announces the publication of his second book, a 'best of' collection of 45 articles from his Joel on Software blog. He humorously describes the book's absurdly long official title and pitches it as useful for winning arguments with colleagues by physical force. He notes some misplaced Amazon reviews and asks readers to write accurate ones. The post includes brief postscripts about the book hitting #1 on Amazon in Computers, a reader dinner in Rome, and an unrelated observation about manual vs. automatic transmissions.
key_points: The book is a curated 'best of' collection from the Joel on Software archive, with 45 articles chosen for timelessness plus minor updates and postscripts
Spolsky emphasizes accessibility and affordability, keeping the price under $17 and positioning it as a physical complement to the free website
He leverages his reader community to fix an Amazon review problem where unrelated Oracle book reviews appeared on his listing
The post exemplifies Spolsky's signature style of mixing substantive announcements with humor and random tangential observations
money_quotes: The biggest advantage of the book is that when you throw it at your colleague's head after a very frustrating argument about whether to throw away all your code and start over from scratch, it makes more of an impact than a URL.
So buy several copies and keep them handy for winning arguments at work.
For some reason there are already four reviews of this book up at Amazon.com which don't make any sense; they look like reviews of an Oracle book I'd like to read.

The only case I know of where a manual transmission beats a good automatic transmission is when you're driving on the highway, and you know that sometime soon you're going to have to pass somebody, so you downshift to third gear to get ready to accelerate.
An automatic transmission can't read your mind, so it stays in 4th or 5th, and has to downshift when you floor the accelerator, thus creating a temporary hesitation.
key_insight: Spolsky treats his book launch as an extension of his community-driven blog, using humor and reader engagement rather than conventional marketing to promote a curated collection of his most enduring software development writing.
